en.extract:
Nutting's flycatcher (Myiarchus nuttingi) is a passerine bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found from Mexico to Costa Rica.


Taxonomy and systematics
Nutting's flycatcher has three subspecies, the nominate M. n. nuttingi (Ridgway, 1882), M. n. inquietus (Salvin & Godman, 1889), and M. n. flavidior (van Rossem, 1936). Some authors have suggested that M. n. flavidior deserves species status due to some differences in vocalizations and plumage, but the American Ornithological Society did not accept a proposal to separate it. The Clements taxonomy does recognize it as "Nutting's flycatcher (Salvadoran)" within the species. "All three taxa have been thought to occur in both pure and hybrid forms in Oaxaca; [the] complex pattern of zones of contact between races has not been assessed since 1961, and study [is] urgently needed."
Some authors have treated Nutting's flycatcher and the ash-throated flycatcher (M. cinerascens) as conspecific.
The species' English name and specific epithet honor Charles Cleveland Nutting (1858–1927).


Description
Nutting's flycatcher is 18 to 19 cm (7.1 to 7.5 in) long and weighs about 21 to 24 g (0.74 to 0.85 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a drab gray-brown crown that has a slight crest. Their face is otherwise a paler gray. Their upperparts are mostly drab gray-brown with a rufescent tinge on the uppertail coverts. Their wings are browner than the upperparts with rufous outer edges on the primaries and grayish white outer edges on the secondaries and tertials. The wing's greater and median coverts have pale tips that show as two wing bars. Their tail is mostly drab gray-brown with thin rufous edges on all but the outermost feathers. Their throat and breast are gray and their belly and undertail coverts yellow. Subspecies M. n. inquietus is larger than the other two but otherwise like the nominate. M. n. flavidor has a richer yellow belly than the other two. All subspecies have a dark iris, a dark bill, and dark legs and feet. Juveniles have much rufous on the tips of the wing coverts and central tail feathers.


Distribution and habitat
Subspecies M. n. inquietus is the northernmost of the three. It is found in western and central Mexico from Sonora south to Chiapas and east in San Luis Potosí and Hidlago. The nominate subspecies is found in the Mexican interior in Oaxaca and Chiapas and from there south on the interior Pacific slope through Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, and Nicaragua into northwestern Costa Rica's Guanacaste Province. M. n. flavidor is found more coastally on the Pacific slope from Oaxaca and Chiapas south to western Nicaragua.
Nutting's flycatcher inhabits forest and scrublands in the tropical and lower subtropical zones. These include tropical deciduous forest, gallery forest, and virgin and second-growth arid scrub. In northern Central America it also is found in thorn forest and in Costa Rica in more humid forest than further north. In elevation overall it ranges from sea level to 1,800 m (5,900 ft) though only to 1,200 m (3,900 ft) in Costa Rica.


Behavior


Movement
Nutting's flycatcher is a year-round resident throughout its range. It does wander northward and has nested in Arizona. It has also been documented as a vagrant in California and Texas.


Feeding
Nutting's flycatcher feeds on insects and berries. It usually forages from the forest understory to its mid-story, and usually with the foliage. It takes most prey and berries by snatching them while briefly hovering after a sally from a perch. Less often it captures insects in mid-air. It has been observed following army ant swarms.


Breeding
Nutting's flycatcher builds a nest of fur, feathers, plant material, and snake skin. It places it in a cavity in a tree or fence post, usually within about 1.2 m (4 ft) of the ground. Its clutch is three to five eggs. Its breeding season, incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.


Vocalization
The primary call of Nutting's flycatcher is "a low whistle that rises initially and then descends, wEEeeeuuu". Other calls include a "shorter wEEeuup...a whistled whinny...agitated nasal calls, pee'up! or wi'wi'uh!, and a scratchy rrheeaa".


Status
The IUCN has assessed Nutting's flycatcher as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its estimated population of at least 500,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered uncommon to fairly common in northern Central America and uncommon in Costa Rica.


References

''' Nutting's flycatcher''' ('''''Myiarchus nuttingi''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers. It is found from [[Mexico]] to [[Costa Rica]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref>
[[File:Nutting's Flycatcher, El Tuito, Jalisco, Mexico.jpg|thumb|El Tuito, Jalisco, Mexico]]

==Taxonomy and systematics==

Nutting's flycatcher has three subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''M. n. nuttingi'' ([[Robert Ridgway|Ridgway]], 1882), ''M. n. inquietus'' ([[Osbert Salvin|Salvin]] & [[Frederick DuCane Godman|Godman]], 1889), and ''M. n. flavidior'' ([[Adriaan Joseph van Rossem|van Rossem]], 1936). Some authors have suggested that ''M. n. flavidior'' deserves species status due to some differences in vocalizations and plumage, but the [[American Ornithological Society]] did not accept a proposal to separate it.<ref>Howell, S. N. G. (2012). "M-M-M-Maybe you just ain't seen Nutting yet?" ''Neotropical Birding'' 10:14–17.</ref><ref name=54Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Richard C. Banks, F. Keith Barker, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-fourth supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2013, vol. 130(3):11 retrieved March 25, 2024</ref> The [[Clements taxonomy]] does recognize it as "Nutting's flycatcher (Salvadoran)" within the species.<ref name=Clements2024>Clements, J. F., P.C. Rasmussen, T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, A. Spencer, S. M. Billerman, B. L. Sullivan, M. Smith, and C. L. Wood. 2024. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2024. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved October 23, 2024</ref> "All three taxa have been thought to occur in both pure and hybrid forms in [[Oaxaca]]; [the] complex pattern of zones of contact between races has not been assessed since 1961, and study [is] urgently needed."<ref name=NUFL-BOW>Joseph, L. (2020). Nutting's Flycatcher (''Myiarchus nuttingi''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.nutfly.01 retrieved July 25, 2025</ref>

Some authors have treated Nutting's flycatcher and the [[ash-throated flycatcher]] (''M. cinerascens'') as [[conspecific]].<ref name=NUFL-BOW/>

The species' English name and [[specific epithet]] honor [[Charles Cleveland Nutting]] (1858–1927).<ref>{{cite web |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=nuttingi |title=The Key to Scientific Names |editor-last=Jobling |editor-first=James A. |date=2025 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=July 25, 2025 }}</ref>

==Description==

Nutting's flycatcher is {{convert|18|to|19|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|21|to|24|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a drab gray-brown crown that has a slight crest. Their face is otherwise a paler gray. Their upperparts are mostly drab gray-brown with a rufescent tinge on the uppertail [[coverts]]. Their wings are browner than the upperparts with rufous outer edges on the [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]] and grayish white outer edges on the [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]] and [[tertials]]. The wing's greater and median coverts have pale tips that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their tail is mostly drab gray-brown with thin rufous edges on all but the outermost feathers. Their throat and breast are gray and their belly and undertail coverts yellow. Subspecies ''M. n. inquietus'' is larger than the other two but otherwise like the nominate. ''M. n. flavidor'' has a richer yellow belly than the other two. All subspecies have a dark iris, a dark bill, and dark legs and feet. Juveniles have much rufous on the tips of the wing coverts and central tail feathers.<ref name=NUFL-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

Subspecies ''M. n. inquietus'' is the northernmost of the three. It is found in western and central Mexico from [[Sonora]] south to [[Chiapas]] and east in [[San Luis Potosí]] and [[Hidalgo (state)|Hidlago]]. The nominate subspecies is found in the Mexican interior in Oaxaca and Chiapas and from there south on the interior Pacific slope through Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, and Nicaragua into northwestern Costa Rica's [[Guanacaste Province]]. ''M. n. flavidor'' is found more coastally on the Pacific slope from Oaxaca and Chiapas south to western Nicaragua.<ref name=NUFL-BOW/>

Nutting's flycatcher inhabits forest and scrublands in the tropical and lower subtropical zones. These include tropical deciduous forest, [[gallery forest]], and virgin and second-growth arid scrub.<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | pages =404–405 }}</ref> In northern Central America it also is found in [[thorn forest]] and in Costa Rica in more humid forest than further north.<ref name=Fagan>{{cite book | last1 =Fagan | first1 =Jesse | last2=Komar |first2=Oliver | title =Field Guide to Birds of Northern Central America | publisher =Houghton Mifflin Harcourt | series =Peterson Field Guides | date =2016 | location =Boston | pages =266–267 |isbn=978-0-544-37326-6 }}</ref><ref name=Garrigues>{{cite book |last1=Garrigues |first1=Richard |last2=Dean |first2=Robert |date=2007 |title=The Birds of Costa Rica |location=Ithaca |publisher=Zona Tropical/Comstock/Cornell University Press |pages=208–209 |isbn=978-0-8014-7373-9 }}</ref> In elevation overall it ranges from sea level to {{convert|1800|m|ft|abbr=on}}<ref name=AOU1998/> though only to {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Costa Rica.<ref name=Garrigues/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

Nutting's flycatcher is a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=AOU1998/> It does wander northward and has nested in Arizona.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://arizonabirdcommittee.org/ABC/lists/state_list_2024.html |title=Checklist of the Birds of Arizona |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=April 1, 2023 |website=Arizona Bird Committee |access-date=July 25, 2025 }}</ref> It has also been documented as a [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] in California<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.californiabirds.org/checklist.asp |title=Official California Checklist |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=July 7, 2025 |website=California Bird Records Committee |access-date=July 25, 2025 }}</ref> and [[Texas]].<ref name="northamerica">{{cite book |isbn=9780691117966 |title=Rare Birds of North America |first1=Steve |last1 = Howell |first2=Ian |last2=Lewington |first3=Will |last3=Russel |page=349 |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2014}}</ref>

===Feeding===

Nutting's flycatcher feeds on insects and berries. It usually forages from the forest understory to its mid-story, and usually with the foliage. It takes most prey and berries by snatching them while briefly hovering after a sally from a perch. Less often it captures insects in mid-air. It has been observed following [[army ant]] swarms.<ref name=NUFL-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nutting's flycatcher builds a nest of fur, feathers, plant material, and snake skin. It places it in a cavity in a tree or fence post, usually within about {{convert|1.2|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} of the ground. Its clutch is three to five eggs. Its breeding season, incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.<ref name=NUFL-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Myiarchus-nuttingi |species=Nutting's flycatcher}}

===Vocalization===

The primary call of Nutting's flycatcher is "a low whistle that rises initially and then descends, ''wEEeeeuuu''". Other calls include a "shorter ''wEEeuup''...a whistled whinny...agitated nasal calls, ''pee'up!'' or ''wi'wi'uh!'', and a scratchy ''rrheeaa''".<ref name=Fagan/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed Nutting's flycatcher as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its estimated population of at least 500,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered uncommon to fairly common in northern Central America and uncommon in Costa Rica.<ref name=Fagan/><ref name=Garrigues/>
